Trie.prompt_notes: Use a baseline trie with commands for insert, search, and starts_with over lowercase English words. Keep the input format command-based and consistent across all four languages.
Trie.canonical_problem: Maintain a lowercase-English trie that supports insert, exact search, and prefix search commands.
Trie.input_contract: Command-based dictionary input: q followed by q commands over lowercase words, such as insert, search, and starts_with.

KMP.prompt_notes: Implement the classical Knuth-Morris-Pratt string matching algorithm using the prefix-function (LPS) preprocessing step. Use command-free input with one text and one pattern per run, and document clearly that the algorithm reports all match starting indices.
KMP.canonical_problem: Given one text and one pattern, report all starting indices where the pattern occurs in the text using classical KMP preprocessing and matching.
KMP.input_contract: Exact string matching input: one text string and one pattern string per run.

Z-Algorithm.prompt_notes: Implement exact string matching via the classical Z-algorithm over pattern + delimiter + text. Use the same one-text one-pattern input contract as KMP so the family remains directly comparable, and report all match starting indices in the text.
Z-Algorithm.canonical_problem: Given one text and one pattern, report all starting indices where the pattern occurs in the text using the Z-algorithm.
Z-Algorithm.input_contract: Exact string matching input: one text string and one pattern string per run.

Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash.prompt_notes: Implement single-pattern exact string matching with a rolling hash in the classical Rabin-Karp style. Use the same one-text one-pattern input contract as KMP and Z-Algorithm, report all match starting indices, and make collision handling explicit in the documentation.
Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash.canonical_problem: Given one text and one pattern, report all starting indices where the pattern occurs in the text using Rabin-Karp rolling hashes with explicit collision verification.
Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash.input_contract: Exact string matching input: one text string and one pattern string per run.

Manacher.prompt_notes: Use Manacher's algorithm on a single input string and make the problem statement explicit about whether the runner returns the longest palindromic substring, its bounds, or palindrome radii. Keep the implementation centered on the classical linear-time radius computation.
Manacher.canonical_problem: Given a single string, compute the longest palindromic substring using Manacher's linear-time palindrome-radius technique.
Manacher.input_contract: Single-string palindrome input: one string per run.

Aho-Corasick.prompt_notes: Implement multi-pattern exact string matching with the classical Aho-Corasick automaton. Use one text plus multiple patterns as input, build trie + failure links explicitly, and make the output contract precise about whether matches are reported by pattern, by text position, or both.
Aho-Corasick.canonical_problem: Given one text and multiple patterns, report exact pattern occurrences in the text using the Aho-Corasick automaton.
Aho-Corasick.input_contract: Multi-pattern matching input: pattern count, that many patterns, then one text string.

Suffix Array.prompt_notes: Define clearly whether this topic delivers only suffix-array construction or also LCP construction, and keep the chosen baseline algorithm explicit in the docs. Avoid leaving the output contract ambiguous between structural arrays and query-style tasks.
Suffix Array.canonical_problem: Given one string, construct its suffix array as the lexicographically sorted list of suffix starting indices.
Suffix Array.input_contract: Single-string suffix-structure input: one string per run.

Suffix Automaton.prompt_notes: Make the baseline application explicit before automation runs: for example, build the suffix automaton for one string and compute a canonical derived quantity such as the number of distinct substrings. Do not leave the topic as only a raw state-machine builder without a fixed observable output.
Suffix Automaton.canonical_problem: Given one string, build its suffix automaton and compute one canonical derived result such as the number of distinct substrings.
Suffix Automaton.input_contract: Single-string automaton input: one string per run.

Depth-First Search.prompt_notes: Treat DFS as one parent topic with recursive and iterative variants over the same graph input contract. Keep shared inputs at the parent and make the traversal-order/output contract explicit so both child variants are directly comparable.
Depth-First Search.canonical_problem: Traverse a graph from a source vertex using depth-first search through recursive and iterative sibling variants.
Depth-First Search.input_contract: Graph traversal input: n, m, source, then m edges in adjacency-list form with one shared parent dataset.

Breadth-First Search.prompt_notes: Use the classical single-source BFS baseline on an unweighted graph. Keep the output contract explicit: traversal order, distance array, or both, and keep it consistent across languages.
Breadth-First Search.canonical_problem: Traverse an unweighted graph from a source vertex and compute shortest-path layer distances with breadth-first search.
Breadth-First Search.input_contract: Unweighted graph input: n, m, source, then m edges.

Dijkstra.prompt_notes: Use the classical single-source shortest-path baseline on a graph with non-negative edge weights. Keep the priority-queue formulation explicit and make the output contract the final distance array from one source.
Dijkstra.canonical_problem: Given a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights and a source, compute shortest-path distances with Dijkstra's algorithm.
Dijkstra.input_contract: Weighted graph input: n, m, source, then m weighted edges.

Topological Sort.prompt_notes: Treat the existing topic as the Kahn-style baseline topological sort over a DAG. Keep the output contract as one valid topological ordering and document cycle detection behavior clearly.
Topological Sort.canonical_problem: Given a directed acyclic graph, produce one valid topological ordering using Kahn's algorithm.
Topological Sort.input_contract: Directed graph input: n, m, then m directed edges.

Disjoint Set Union.prompt_notes: Keep DSU as a standalone data-structure topic even though it is heavily used in graph algorithms. Use command-based union/find queries, make path compression and union-by-rank or union-by-size explicit, and document its graph relevance in the problem statement.
Disjoint Set Union.canonical_problem: Maintain dynamic disjoint sets under union and connectivity queries using path compression and a union heuristic.
Disjoint Set Union.input_contract: Command input: n, q, then q union/find or connectivity commands over element indices.

Bellman-Ford.prompt_notes: Use the classical single-source shortest-path baseline with possible negative weights and explicit negative-cycle detection reachable from the source. Keep the output contract clear about what is reported when a negative cycle is found.
Bellman-Ford.canonical_problem: Given a weighted directed graph and a source, compute shortest-path distances while detecting reachable negative cycles using Bellman-Ford.
Bellman-Ford.input_contract: Weighted directed graph input: n, m, source, then m weighted directed edges.

Floyd-Warshall.prompt_notes: Use the classical all-pairs shortest-path dynamic-programming baseline over a dense adjacency matrix or edge list that expands into one. Keep negative-cycle discussion explicit and make the matrix-style output contract clear.
Floyd-Warshall.canonical_problem: Compute all-pairs shortest-path distances with Floyd-Warshall on a weighted graph.
Floyd-Warshall.input_contract: Weighted graph input: n, m, then m weighted edges, with output as an n x n distance structure.

Kruskal Minimum Spanning Tree.prompt_notes: Use the classical minimum-spanning-tree baseline on an undirected weighted graph. Keep DSU usage explicit, document how disconnected graphs are handled, and make the output contract either total MST weight or the chosen edges plus total weight.
Kruskal Minimum Spanning Tree.canonical_problem: Given an undirected weighted graph, compute a minimum spanning tree with Kruskal's algorithm.
Kruskal Minimum Spanning Tree.input_contract: Undirected weighted graph input: n, m, then m weighted edges.

Prim Minimum Spanning Tree.prompt_notes: Use the classical minimum-spanning-tree baseline on an undirected weighted graph, preferably with an adjacency-list plus priority-queue formulation. Keep the output contract aligned with Kruskal for comparability.
Prim Minimum Spanning Tree.canonical_problem: Given an undirected weighted graph, compute a minimum spanning tree with Prim's algorithm.
Prim Minimum Spanning Tree.input_contract: Undirected weighted graph input: n, m, then m weighted edges.

Bidirectional BFS.prompt_notes: Use the canonical shortest-path baseline in an unweighted graph with one source and one target. Keep the implementation explicit about frontiers grown from both sides and document what is returned when the target is unreachable.
Bidirectional BFS.canonical_problem: Given an unweighted graph, a source, and a target, compute the shortest-path distance using bidirectional BFS.
Bidirectional BFS.input_contract: Unweighted graph input: n, m, source, target, then m edges.

A* Search.prompt_notes: Use a 4-direction grid shortest-path baseline with blocked cells and Manhattan heuristic. Keep the heuristic admissibility discussion explicit and make the output contract the shortest path length or unreachable status.
A* Search.canonical_problem: Given a grid with blocked cells, a start, and a goal, compute the shortest path length using A* with a Manhattan-distance heuristic.
A* Search.input_contract: Grid search input: rows, cols, grid cells, start coordinate, goal coordinate.

Shortest Path in DAG.prompt_notes: Use the classical topological-order relaxation baseline on a weighted DAG from one source. Keep the output contract as the final distance array and contrast it with Dijkstra and Bellman-Ford in the docs.
Shortest Path in DAG.canonical_problem: Given a weighted DAG and a source, compute shortest-path distances by relaxing edges in topological order.
Shortest Path in DAG.input_contract: Weighted DAG input: n, m, source, then m weighted directed edges.

Topological Sort, DFS-based.prompt_notes: Treat this as a distinct single-topic counterpart to the existing Kahn-based topological sort. Use the same DAG input contract where practical, make the reverse-postorder construction explicit, and document cycle-handling expectations.
Topological Sort, DFS-based.canonical_problem: Given a directed acyclic graph, produce one valid topological ordering using DFS reverse postorder.
Topological Sort, DFS-based.input_contract: Directed graph input: n, m, then m directed edges.

Strongly Connected Components, Kosaraju.pr_title: Add strongly connected components with Kosaraju
Strongly Connected Components, Kosaraju.prompt_notes: Use the classical two-pass SCC decomposition over a directed graph. Make the transposed-graph pass explicit and keep the output contract as component identifiers or grouped vertices in a deterministic order.
Strongly Connected Components, Kosaraju.canonical_problem: Given a directed graph, decompose it into strongly connected components using Kosaraju's two-pass algorithm.
Strongly Connected Components, Kosaraju.input_contract: Directed graph input: n, m, then m directed edges.

Strongly Connected Components, Tarjan.pr_title: Add strongly connected components with Tarjan
Strongly Connected Components, Tarjan.prompt_notes: Use the classical single-pass low-link SCC decomposition over a directed graph. Keep stack membership and low-link semantics explicit and align the output contract with the Kosaraju topic where practical.
Strongly Connected Components, Tarjan.canonical_problem: Given a directed graph, decompose it into strongly connected components using Tarjan's low-link algorithm.
Strongly Connected Components, Tarjan.input_contract: Directed graph input: n, m, then m directed edges.

Bridges.prompt_notes: Use the classical DFS low-link bridge-finding baseline on an undirected graph. Keep the root/parent-edge handling explicit and make the output contract the list of bridge edges in a deterministic order.
Bridges.canonical_problem: Given an undirected graph, find all bridge edges using DFS timestamps and low-link values.
Bridges.input_contract: Undirected graph input: n, m, then m edges.

Articulation Points.prompt_notes: Use the classical DFS low-link articulation-point baseline on an undirected graph. Keep the root-child-count rule explicit and make the output contract the set of articulation vertices in a deterministic order.
Articulation Points.canonical_problem: Given an undirected graph, find all articulation points using DFS timestamps and low-link values.
Articulation Points.input_contract: Undirected graph input: n, m, then m edges.

Eulerian Path, Hierholzer.prompt_notes: Use an undirected multigraph baseline and make the existence conditions explicit before constructing the path with Hierholzer's algorithm. Keep the output contract as one Eulerian path/circuit or an explicit impossible result.
Eulerian Path, Hierholzer.canonical_problem: Given an undirected graph that may contain parallel edges, construct an Eulerian path or circuit with Hierholzer's algorithm when one exists.
Eulerian Path, Hierholzer.input_contract: Undirected multigraph input: n, m, then m edges.

DAG Dynamic Programming.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until one canonical baseline is fixed. The strongest candidate is longest path in a weighted DAG or counting paths from one source to one sink, but the topic should not stay as an unbounded DP pattern name.
DAG Dynamic Programming.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: solve longest path or path-count DP on a DAG by processing vertices in topological order.
DAG Dynamic Programming.input_contract: Candidate DAG DP input: directed acyclic graph with optional source, sink, and edge/node weights depending on the chosen baseline.

Binary Tree Traversals.prompt_notes: The current repo layout is nested by traversal order (Preorder/Inorder/Postorder) and then by execution style (recursive/iterative). Do not treat this as a runnable single entry until the catalog explicitly decides whether the delivery unit should be one parent topic with nested variants or multiple order-specific parent topics.
Binary Tree Traversals.canonical_problem: Traverse a binary tree in preorder, inorder, and postorder while contrasting recursive and iterative implementations.
Binary Tree Traversals.input_contract: Binary-tree traversal input shared across traversal-order families, typically a serialized rooted binary tree.

Rooted Tree Traversals, preorder.prompt_notes: Keep this distinct from binary-tree preorder traversal. Use a general rooted tree adjacency-list baseline and return the preorder visitation order from a designated root.
Rooted Tree Traversals, preorder.canonical_problem: Given a rooted tree, output its preorder traversal order from the designated root.
Rooted Tree Traversals, preorder.input_contract: Rooted-tree input: n, root, then n-1 edges or parent links defining the tree.

Rooted Tree Traversals, postorder.prompt_notes: Keep this distinct from binary-tree postorder traversal. Use a general rooted tree adjacency-list baseline and return the postorder visitation order from a designated root.
Rooted Tree Traversals, postorder.canonical_problem: Given a rooted tree, output its postorder traversal order from the designated root.
Rooted Tree Traversals, postorder.input_contract: Rooted-tree input: n, root, then n-1 edges or parent links defining the tree.

Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.pr_title: Add binary tree level order traversal
Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.prompt_notes: Use the canonical queue-based breadth-first traversal over a serialized binary tree. Make the output contract explicit as either flat visitation order with level markers or grouped levels, and keep it consistent across languages.
Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.canonical_problem: Given a binary tree, output its level-order traversal using a queue.
Binary Tree Level Order Traversal.input_contract: Serialized binary-tree input with null markers or equivalent child placeholders.

Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.checklist_label: Binary Search Tree Search and Insert
Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.display_name: Binary Search Tree Search and Insert
Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.pr_title: Add binary search tree search and insert
Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.prompt_notes: Use the classical unbalanced BST baseline with command-based insert and search operations over integer keys. Keep the output contract explicit for successful/unsuccessful searches and avoid mixing balancing logic into this topic.
Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.canonical_problem: Maintain an unbalanced binary search tree under insert and exact-search queries.
Binary Search Tree Search and Insert.input_contract: Command input: q followed by insert/search commands over integer keys.

Balanced Binary Search Tree.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until the baseline balancing scheme is fixed explicitly. The main open decision is AVL vs Red-Black Tree, and that choice affects rotations, invariants, and problem framing enough that it belongs in catalog design, not generation time.
Balanced Binary Search Tree.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: maintain an ordered set or map under insert/search operations using one explicit self-balancing BST scheme.
Balanced Binary Search Tree.input_contract: Command input: q followed by insert/search or ordered-query commands over integer keys.

Validate Binary Search Tree.prompt_notes: Use a serialized binary-tree input and validate the BST property with explicit min/max or inorder constraints. Make duplicate-key policy explicit in the docs and keep it consistent across implementations.
Validate Binary Search Tree.canonical_problem: Given a binary tree, determine whether it satisfies the binary-search-tree ordering property.
Validate Binary Search Tree.input_contract: Serialized binary-tree input with null markers or equivalent child placeholders.

Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.checklist_label: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.display_name: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.pr_title: Add lowest common ancestor in binary tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.prompt_notes: Use the classical single-query recursive baseline on a binary tree. Keep the node-identification contract explicit, such as querying by unique node values or by indexed nodes in the serialized tree.
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.canonical_problem: Given a binary tree and two target nodes, compute their lowest common ancestor.
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Tree.input_contract: Serialized binary-tree input plus two target node identifiers.

Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.checklist_label: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search Tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.display_name: Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search Tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.pr_title: Add lowest common ancestor in binary search tree
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.prompt_notes: Use the BST-ordering property explicitly rather than falling back to the generic binary-tree LCA algorithm. Keep the input contract similar to the binary-tree LCA topic for easy comparison.
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.canonical_problem: Given a binary search tree and two target nodes, compute their lowest common ancestor using BST ordering.
Lowest Common Ancestor in Binary Search.input_contract: Serialized BST input plus two target node identifiers.

Binary Lifting.prompt_notes: Use rooted-tree ancestor queries as the canonical baseline so the topic has a concrete observable contract independent of LCA. A good baseline is multiple kth-ancestor queries after O(n log n) preprocessing.
Binary Lifting.canonical_problem: Given a rooted tree, preprocess jump pointers so kth-ancestor queries can be answered in O(log n).
Binary Lifting.input_contract: Rooted-tree input: n, root, parent links or edges, q, then q ancestor queries.

Tree Diameter.prompt_notes: Use the classical two-DFS/BFS unweighted-tree baseline unless weighted diameter is explicitly chosen later. Keep the output contract as diameter length and, if practical, the endpoint pair.
Tree Diameter.canonical_problem: Given an unweighted tree, compute the length of its diameter using two traversals.
Tree Diameter.input_contract: Tree input: n followed by n-1 undirected edges.

Subtree Dynamic Programming.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until one canonical DP objective is fixed. Candidate baselines include subtree sizes, subtree sums, or maximum independent set on a tree, but the catalog should not leave the topic as a broad method name.
Subtree Dynamic Programming.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: compute one subtree-aggregated DP quantity over a rooted tree using postorder processing.
Subtree Dynamic Programming.input_contract: Candidate rooted-tree input: n, root, edges, and optional node weights depending on the chosen DP objective.

Rerooting Dynamic Programming.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until one canonical rerooting objective is fixed. Sum of distances from every node or subtree-based all-roots aggregation are strong candidates, but the delivery unit needs a single clear problem contract.
Rerooting Dynamic Programming.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: compute an all-roots tree DP quantity by combining one subtree pass with one rerooting pass.
Rerooting Dynamic Programming.input_contract: Candidate rooted-tree input: n, edges, and optional node weights depending on the chosen rerooting objective.

Fenwick Tree.prompt_notes: Use the existing baseline of point-add updates plus range-sum queries via prefix sums. Keep the one-dimensional array/query contract explicit and document the comparison to Segment Tree.
Fenwick Tree.canonical_problem: Maintain an array under point-add updates and range-sum queries using a Fenwick tree.
Fenwick Tree.input_contract: Array-query input: n, q, initial array values, then add/sum operations.

Segment Tree.prompt_notes: Use the existing baseline of point-add updates plus range-sum queries with an iterative bottom-up segment tree. Keep the array/query contract aligned with Fenwick Tree so the two topics remain directly comparable.
Segment Tree.canonical_problem: Maintain an array under point-add updates and range-sum queries using an iterative segment tree.
Segment Tree.input_contract: Array-query input: n, q, initial array values, then add/sum operations.

Lazy Segment Tree.prompt_notes: Use the canonical range-add plus range-sum baseline so the lazy propagation mechanism is the main teaching point. Keep the contract deliberately close to Segment Tree while expanding the update class from point updates to interval updates.
Lazy Segment Tree.canonical_problem: Maintain an array under range-add updates and range-sum queries using lazy propagation on a segment tree.
Lazy Segment Tree.input_contract: Array-query input: n, q, initial array values, then range-add and range-sum operations.

Sparse Table.prompt_notes: Use range minimum query as the canonical sparse-table baseline. Keep the topic explicit about static preprocessing, idempotent queries, and why the structure differs from Fenwick and Segment Tree.
Sparse Table.canonical_problem: Preprocess an array so range minimum queries can be answered in O(1) with a sparse table.
Sparse Table.input_contract: Static query input: n, q, array values, then q inclusive range-minimum queries.

Binary Search.prompt_notes: Treat binary search as one parent topic with the existing exact-match and boundary-search variants. Keep shared inputs at the parent level, keep variant-specific docs and runners below it, and document clearly why exact lookup and lower/upper-bound style queries belong to the same family.
Binary Search.canonical_problem: Given a sorted array, support exact-match and boundary-style binary-search queries as sibling variants of one topic family.
Binary Search.input_contract: Sorted-array search inputs with shared parent-level datasets and variant-specific query semantics.

Binary Search on Answer.prompt_notes: Use one canonical monotone-feasibility baseline: partition an array into at most k contiguous groups while minimizing the maximum group sum. Make the feasibility predicate explicit and document that the algorithm searches over answer space rather than over stored array indices.
Binary Search on Answer.canonical_problem: Given an array and a partition limit k, minimize the maximum segment sum by binary searching the answer and checking monotone feasibility.
Binary Search on Answer.input_contract: Partition-search input: n, k, then n non-negative integers.

Ternary Search.prompt_notes: Use a discrete unimodal-array baseline so the runner has a concrete observable output. Given a unimodal array, return the index and value of its maximum, and document that this is the discrete-search analogue of ternary search over unimodal functions.
Ternary Search.canonical_problem: Given a unimodal array, find the index of its maximum with discrete ternary search.
Ternary Search.input_contract: Unimodal-array input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers that increase then decrease.

Two Pointers.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until the canonical baseline problem is locked. The likely candidate is pair-sum in a sorted array, but the catalog should not silently collapse the broader two-pointer pattern into one problem without explicit approval.
Two Pointers.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: find whether a sorted array contains a pair whose sum equals a target using opposing pointers.
Two Pointers.input_contract: Candidate sorted-array pair-sum input: n, target, then n sorted integers.

Fast and Slow Pointers.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until the baseline structure is fixed. Floyd cycle detection over a next-index array is a strong candidate because it avoids introducing linked-list infrastructure too early while preserving the tortoise-hare algorithm.
Fast and Slow Pointers.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: detect and locate a cycle in a functional graph or next-index structure using Floyd's tortoise-hare method.
Fast and Slow Pointers.input_contract: Candidate next-index input: n followed by n next pointers encoded as indices or sentinel values.

Sliding Window.prompt_notes: Treat sliding window as one parent topic with fixed-size and variable-size variants. Keep the family-level explanation at the parent and keep each child variant tied to one explicit baseline problem so the pair remains comparable without collapsing into one vague pattern page.
Sliding Window.canonical_problem: Teach the sliding-window family through two sibling variants: fixed-size maximum-sum windows and variable-size longest-valid windows.
Sliding Window.input_contract: Shared array-window datasets at the parent level with variant-specific query and constraint interpretation.

Prefix Sum.prompt_notes: Implement one-dimensional prefix sums for static range-sum queries over signed 64-bit integers. Keep the input contract query-based and document the preprocessing/query tradeoff explicitly.
Prefix Sum.canonical_problem: Preprocess an array so that multiple inclusive range-sum queries can be answered in O(1) after linear preprocessing.
Prefix Sum.input_contract: Range-query input: n, array values, q, then q inclusive index ranges.

Suffix Sum.prompt_notes: Use the same style of static range-query explanation as Prefix Sum, but oriented from the array tail. Keep the topic explicit about why suffix sums are included separately instead of only being a trivial code mirror.
Suffix Sum.canonical_problem: Preprocess an array so that suffix-sum and tail-oriented range-sum queries can be answered in O(1) after linear preprocessing.
Suffix Sum.input_contract: Suffix-query input: n, array values, q, then q suffix or tail-range queries.

Difference Array.prompt_notes: Implement one-dimensional difference arrays for batched range updates followed by one reconstruction pass. Keep the input command-based and clearly contrast this topic with Prefix Sum.
Difference Array.canonical_problem: Apply multiple inclusive range-add updates efficiently and reconstruct the final array with one prefix-style sweep.
Difference Array.input_contract: Range-update input: n, initial array, q, then q update triples (l, r, delta).

Interval Merge.prompt_notes: Use the canonical merge-overlapping-intervals baseline. Sort by interval start, merge overlaps greedily, and make the output contract precise as the normalized non-overlapping interval list.
Interval Merge.canonical_problem: Given a list of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals into a normalized non-overlapping list.
Interval Merge.input_contract: Interval input: n followed by n closed intervals (l, r).

Sweep Line.prompt_notes: Do not make this runnable until one baseline event model is locked. Meeting-room overlap counting or maximum concurrent intervals are strong candidates, but the queue should not generate a broad 'sweep line' topic without a fixed event contract.
Sweep Line.canonical_problem: Candidate baseline: given intervals, compute the maximum number of simultaneously active intervals using event sorting.
Sweep Line.input_contract: Candidate event input: n followed by n intervals interpreted as start/end events.

Boyer-Moore Majority Vote.prompt_notes: Use the classical majority-element baseline with an optional verification pass. Make the contract explicit about whether the input guarantees a majority element or whether the runner must verify the candidate before reporting it.
Boyer-Moore Majority Vote.canonical_problem: Given an array, identify the majority element if one exists using Boyer-Moore majority vote.
Boyer-Moore Majority Vote.input_contract: Array-majority input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Quickselect.prompt_notes: Use the canonical kth-smallest selection baseline. Keep the pivot strategy explicit, return the kth smallest value under 1-based or 0-based indexing that is clearly documented, and include a challenging input that stresses poor partition behavior.
Quickselect.canonical_problem: Given an unsorted array and k, return the kth smallest element using quickselect.
Quickselect.input_contract: Selection input: n, k, then n signed 64-bit integers.

Matrix Multiplication.prompt_notes: Treat matrix multiplication as one parent family with classical and blocked variants. Use one shared dense-matrix input contract, keep result formatting identical across variants, and document the systems rationale for including the blocked variant rather than presenting both as unrelated topics.
Matrix Multiplication.canonical_problem: Multiply dense matrices with two sibling variants: classical cubic multiplication and blocked multiplication over the same input contract.
Matrix Multiplication.input_contract: Dense-matrix input: dimensions for A and B followed by row-major integer matrix entries, with classical and blocked variants sharing the same data.

Kadane's Algorithm.prompt_notes: Use the classical maximum-subarray baseline and report both the maximum sum and its subarray bounds when practical. Keep the topic explicit about how all-negative arrays are handled.
Kadane's Algorithm.canonical_problem: Given an array, compute the maximum-sum contiguous subarray with Kadane's linear-time scan.
Kadane's Algorithm.input_contract: Array-scan input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

2D Prefix Sum.prompt_notes: Extend Prefix Sum to a static 2D rectangle-sum baseline. Use a matrix plus rectangle queries, keep the inclusion-exclusion formula explicit in the docs, and align the input style with the one-dimensional query topics.
2D Prefix Sum.canonical_problem: Preprocess a matrix so that multiple axis-aligned rectangle-sum queries can be answered in O(1) after O(nm) preprocessing.
2D Prefix Sum.input_contract: Matrix-query input: rows, cols, matrix values, q, then q rectangle queries.

Insertion Sort.prompt_notes: Implement classical insertion sort over signed 64-bit integers. Use a simple array input format and document clearly that the algorithm is in-place, stable, and quadratic in the worst case.
Insertion Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of signed 64-bit integers in non-decreasing order using classical insertion sort.
Insertion Sort.input_contract: Simple array sort input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Selection Sort.prompt_notes: Implement classical selection sort over signed 64-bit integers. Use a simple array input format and document clearly that the algorithm is in-place, not stable in its standard form, and quadratic in the worst case.
Selection Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of signed 64-bit integers in non-decreasing order using classical selection sort.
Selection Sort.input_contract: Simple array sort input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Merge Sort.prompt_notes: Implement top-down merge sort over signed 64-bit integers. Use a simple array input format and document clearly that the algorithm is stable, uses auxiliary memory, and guarantees O(n log n) time.
Merge Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of signed 64-bit integers in non-decreasing order using top-down merge sort.
Merge Sort.input_contract: Simple array sort input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Quick Sort.prompt_notes: Implement classical quick sort over signed 64-bit integers with an explicit pivot strategy documented in the problem statement. Use a simple array input format and include a challenging input that stresses poor partition behavior.
Quick Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of signed 64-bit integers in non-decreasing order using classical quick sort with one documented pivot strategy.
Quick Sort.input_contract: Simple array sort input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Heap Sort.prompt_notes: Implement in-place heap sort over signed 64-bit integers using a max-heap. Use a simple array input format and document clearly that the algorithm is not stable but guarantees O(n log n) time.
Heap Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of signed 64-bit integers in non-decreasing order using in-place heap sort with a max-heap.
Heap Sort.input_contract: Simple array sort input: n followed by n signed 64-bit integers.

Counting Sort.prompt_notes: Implement counting sort for bounded integers with the input explicitly providing the value range. Keep the format simple, validate range assumptions carefully, and document that the algorithm trades memory for linear-time sorting under bounded domains.
Counting Sort.canonical_problem: Sort a bounded integer array in non-decreasing order using counting sort with an explicit declared value range.
Counting Sort.input_contract: Range-aware sort input: n, lower bound, upper bound, then n bounded integers inside that inclusive range.

Radix Sort.prompt_notes: Implement least-significant-digit radix sort for non-negative integers using a stable counting-sort pass per digit. Use an input format that makes the non-negative constraint explicit and document that stability is required across digit passes.
Radix Sort.canonical_problem: Sort an array of non-negative integers in non-decreasing order using least-significant-digit radix sort with stable digit passes.
Radix Sort.input_contract: Non-negative sort input: n followed by n non-negative integers.
